metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Studio Arts 2D 11 advances students' mastery of two-dimensional media including drawing, painting, printmaking, and mixed media. Students develop a personal artistic voice, engage in sustained studio inquiry, and critically analyze two-dimensional art from diverse cultural perspectives. First Peoples 2D art traditions — particularly Northwest Coast formline design — are studied in depth. Students build toward a cohesive portfolio. This course prepares students for Studio Arts 2D 12.
metadata.general_description: An advanced senior 2D visual arts course developing technical mastery, personal artistic voice, studio inquiry, art criticism, and portfolio development across drawing, painting, printmaking, and mixed media.

big_ideas.statement: Technical skill in 2D media enables greater expressive freedom and artistic intentionality.
big_ideas.elaboration: As artists develop mastery of drawing, painting, and printmaking, they gain greater capacity to realize their artistic vision with precision and nuance — and to make deliberate, expressive choices.

big_ideas.statement: Artists develop a personal voice through sustained inquiry, experimentation, and reflection.
big_ideas.elaboration: Artistic identity emerges through the sustained practice of making, questioning, and reflecting. Studio inquiry — the deliberate exploration of ideas, materials, and meaning — is the engine of artistic development.

big_ideas.statement: Visual art communicates ideas, values, and cultural knowledge that transcend language.
big_ideas.elaboration: Two-dimensional art traditions across cultures — from cave paintings to contemporary digital works — carry meaning that cannot be fully captured in words. Visual literacy is as important as verbal literacy in understanding the world.

big_ideas.statement: Art both reflects and challenges the culture and context in which it is created.
big_ideas.elaboration: Understanding 2D art requires understanding its social, historical, and cultural context — who made it, for whom, and in response to what. Art can confirm dominant values or powerfully challenge them.

competencies.raw: Develop technical mastery across 2D media; apply elements and principles with increasing intentionality; develop personal artistic voice through sustained studio inquiry; take purposeful creative risks.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate technical mastery in multiple 2D media — drawing, painting, and printmaking.
I can apply elements and principles of art and design with deliberate intentionality and expressive purpose.
I can develop a personal artistic voice through sustained studio inquiry — exploring ideas, materials, and forms over time.
I can take purposeful creative risks that push my work beyond familiar approaches.

competencies.raw: Analyze artworks using advanced visual arts vocabulary; apply critical feedback to refine studio practice; examine art in social, cultural, historical, and political context.
i_can_statements: I can analyze artworks — including my own — using advanced visual arts vocabulary and critical frameworks.
I can apply detailed critical feedback to make targeted improvements to my studio practice.
I can examine artworks in their social, cultural, and historical contexts, understanding the relationship between art and power.

competencies.raw: Present and share studio work with artistic intention; document artistic process and development; write artist statements that articulate vision and intent.
i_can_statements: I can present and share 2D studio work with clarity about artistic intention and context.
I can document my artistic process — including research, sketches, experiments, and reflections — in a studio journal or process portfolio.
I can write artist statements that clearly articulate my artistic vision, intent, and process.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate ethical responsibility in art-making; engage with First Peoples 2D art traditions; examine how 2D art shapes culture and community.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate ethical responsibility — respecting intellectual property, cultural protocols, and representation.
I can engage with First Peoples 2D art traditions — Northwest Coast formline, contemporary Indigenous art — with depth and respect.
I can examine how 2D art shapes and reflects cultural values, social identity, and community history.

areas.name: Advanced Drawing
topics: Observational drawing: complex still life, portraiture, and figure drawing
Perspective: two-point, three-point, and atmospheric perspective
Expressive and gestural drawing: using line quality and mark-making for emotion
Mixed drawing media: charcoal, ink, pastel, graphite, and conte
Conceptual drawing: developing images from ideas rather than observation alone

areas.name: Painting
areas.raw: Painting techniques, media, and conceptual approaches.
topics: Colour mixing: complex colour relationships, value studies, and colour harmony
Acrylic and oil painting techniques: glazing, impasto, underpainting, and wet-on-wet
Watercolour: wet-on-wet, wet-on-dry, lifting, and masking techniques
Large-scale painting: working with increased scale and compositional complexity
Conceptual and expressive painting: developing series and thematic bodies of work

areas.name: Printmaking
topics: Advanced linocut and woodcut: multi-block reduction printing
Etching and intaglio processes (where equipment allows)
Screen printing: photo emulsion and multi-colour printing
Monoprinting: creating unique prints through non-repeatable processes
Artist's books and zines as printmaking-adjacent forms

areas.name: Art History, Critical Theory, and Portfolio
areas.raw: Art history, critical analysis, and portfolio development.
topics: Critical frameworks: formalism, social criticism, feminist art theory, and postcolonial analysis
First Peoples 2D art traditions: Northwest Coast formline design, principles and contemporary applications
Contemporary 2D artists: working artists from diverse backgrounds and traditions
Portfolio development: curating a cohesive body of work with written reflection
Exhibition planning: installation, labelling, and presenting work in a public context
